title: UNDERSTANDING HTML WITH LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS

structured.summary: The paper explores the application of large language models (LLMs) for understanding HTML, aiming to improve web automation tasks without requiring specialized architectures. It demonstrates that LLMs, particularly those with encoder-decoder architectures like T5, can outperform task-specific models in semantic classification, description generation, and web navigation tasks, using significantly less data, and offers a new open-source HTML dataset for further research.

strengths.point: Effective use of existing LLMs for HTML tasks
strengths.evidence: The paper shows that fine-tuned LLMs can achieve 12% higher accuracy in semantic classification and complete 50% more tasks on MiniWoB using 192x less data.

strengths.point: Contribution of dataset to the research community
strengths.evidence: The authors created and plan to open-source a large-scale HTML dataset distilled from CommonCrawl for further research in description generation.

weaknesses.evidence: The paper applies existing LLM architectures like T5 without introducing new models or task-specific innovations, relying on the capabilities of pre-existing models.
The paper does not provide comprehensive details about fine-tuning processes, data handling, or hyperparameters, making it difficult to reproduce the results.
The experiments focus mainly on the MiniWoB benchmark, without testing on more diverse, real-world HTML datasets to show broader applicability.

questions.question: How do LLMs handle diverse HTML structures in real-world applications?
questions.motivation: Understanding the generalization ability of LLMs across different HTML formats is crucial for real-world applicability beyond the benchmarks used in the study.

critique_points.point_001: Limited novelty in methodology. The paper applies existing LLM architectures like T5 without introducing new models or task-specific innovations, relying on the capabilities of pre-existing models
critique_points.point_002: Lack of detailed methodological explanations. The paper does not provide comprehensive details about fine-tuning processes, data handling, or hyperparameters, making it difficult to reproduce the results
critique_points.point_003: Narrow evaluation scope. The experiments focus mainly on the MiniWoB benchmark, without testing on more diverse, real-world HTML datasets to show broader applicability
